title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | City, state win same-sex benefits case RULING: Workers' gay and lesbian partners don't qualify under current law. By Zaz Hollander Anchorage Daily News (Published: November 18, 2001) The city of Anchorage and the state of Alaska do not have to extend benefits to gay or lesbian partners of employees and retirees, an Anchorage Superior Court judge has ruled. Same-sex couples fall into the same legal category as unmarried male-female couples and neither are entitled to city or state benefits under current law, Judge Stephanie Joannides decided. The ruling Friday marks a disappointing loss for nine same-sex couples who filed a lawsuit demanding health insurance and pension benefits in 1999. Lin Davis, a state Labor Department employee, says she has earned the right to add to her health c…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | | Floatplane narrowly misses whale BREACH: Takeoff incident stuns pilot and two passengers. The Associated Press (Published: August 13, 2001) Juneau -- If Petersburg pilot Rod Judy had taken off from Keku Islands seconds later than he did last week, he might have collided in midair with a humpback whale. But time was on his side that day and gave him a good story instead. Judy, a commercial pilot, flew to the islands about 100 miles south of Juneau last weekend to pick up two U.S. Forest Service employees bound for Petersburg. The floatplane was moving on the water about 50 mph and was just about to lift off when a humpback whale suddenly breached in front of it. "We were just clearing the water, and right dead ahead of us this thing came clear out of the water. We were staring right i…

title: Citizens protest airport expansion
summary: Ted Stevens Anchorage International Airport should not continue to grow at the expense of the city's neighborhoods and parks, community and trails advocates said this week. Nearly 200 people filled a banquet room at the WestCoast International Inn Thursday night to deliver that message and ask questions about the airport's long-range expansion plans. Many meeting participants said they were not impressed with the airport's responses and accused officials of being more concerned with the airport's cargo business than with protecting city parks and neighborhoods. "I see the airport doing what it wants now and reserving the right to do what it wants later," said Randy Virgin, director of the Alaska Center for the Environment. "Until we see some end or limit to (airport growth), this controver…

title: 1st air rage twin gets $86,775 bill
summary: One of the air rage twins arrested in Anchorage in April was sentenced Wednesday to five years probation, restitution of $86,774.92, and 231 hours of community service -- one hour for each person on the plane she caused to be diverted and delayed. Cynthia Mikula also was banned from commercial airplanes for five years, said assistant U.S. Attorney Charlie Brown, except for the flight that will return her to Michigan with her mother as chaperone. Mikula pleaded guilty in federal court to interfering with a flight crew and apologized for her behavior, Brown said. Several of the crew from the flight were in court. Mikula, 22, and her twin sister, Crystal, were accused of smoking, fighting with each other, attacking crew members who tried to calm them, and creating such a disturbance on an Apr…

title: Assembly delays until January acting on ball field proposal
summary: ANCHORAGE Assembly delays until January acting on ball field proposal The Anchorage Assembly Tuesday opted not to take action on a proposal to amend the Far North Bicentennial Park master plan until Jan. 15. The amendment would set the stage for building ball fields on 20 to 25 acres on the southwest corner of the park. Families of the Simonian Little League want to use the park for ball fields by 2004, when they will have to leave their fields on Art Simonian's property. Others oppose razing part of the park. Many say the city should explore other locations for the little league. Tuesday was the third and final night in which the Assembly heard hours of public comments on the subject. -- Anchorage Daily News ANCHORAGE Frontier Printing Services owner indicted on five bank fraud counts Nez…

title: Phillips cleaning up Kuparuk spill
summary: Phillips Alaska Inc. has diked one section of a spill area in the Kuparuk oil field and is preparing to flush the ground with warm water to lift salt and crude oil off the tundra, state officials said. "(The cleanup) is going very smoothly. We're ahead of schedule," said Ed Meggert, head of oil spill response with the state Department of Environmental Conservation. In one of the largest spills ever to hit the North Slope tundra, more than 92,000 gallons of saltwater and crude leaked from a pipeline Sunday night. Phillips personnel discovered the spill at 10:45 p.m. when the pipeline, which ran from Kuparuk's central processing facility to oil production pad 1B, had a sudden drop in pressure. The 10-inch pipeline carries what is known as "produced water," a mixture that is about 97 percent …

title: Sanctuary or Solution?
summary: THE COASTAL PLAIN -- The wind shivers across this expanse of the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge, chasing the snow before it in wafting white lace south toward the Brooks Range. A cold, clear dawn greets Kaktovik on a minus-30 morning in late March. A boat awaits breakup on a lagoon inside the spit that supports the runway for the village and adjacent radar site. (Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) The coastal plain's hard beauty is exceptional, but it is what lies underneath that makes this place controversial: oil. With anywhere from 5.7 billion to 16 billion barrels of oil, the coastal plain may hold the largest undeveloped onshore reserves in North America. For Alaskans, the refuge debate is not a new one. What's changed is that for the first time since the massive Exxon Valdez spill in…

title: Snowmachiners rattle railroad
summary: Wasilla -- As head of security for the Alaska Railroad, Dan Frerich dreads seeing snowmachine trails near the railroad tracks. Even worse, snowmachine tracks between the rails. Over the past 10 years, Frerich has investigated dozens of collisions and near-collisions between snowmobiles and trains from Seward to Fairbanks. No one has been killed while riding a snowmachine on the tracks, according to railroad records. But in the past few years, railroad crews have reported at least five close calls, including four in which trains had to make emergency stops to avoid snowmachines, a maneuver that can derail a train. Last spring, a 27-year-old Anchorage woman riding with her family was badly hurt after her snowmachine got caught on the tracks near downtown Wasilla and she was slammed by a full…

title: Air rage twin set to plead guilty
summary: One of the twin models whose bad behavior forced a nonstop international flight to be diverted to Anchorage in April has agreed to plead guilty to interfering with a flight crew, a federal felony, according to court papers. Cynthia Mikula, 22, will be sentenced after she enters the guilty plea at a hearing in September, according to court records. She faces up to 20 years in prison, a $250,000 fine, a $100 fee, and a three-year term of supervised release. The plea agreement filed in U.S. District Court Monday estimates Mikula's sentence will range between zero and six months. Mikula was originally scheduled to change her plea today. According to court documents, she requested a delay until September because her family will be in Anchorage for the sentencing of her sister, Crystal Mikula, w…

title: Company's new product favors Alaska
summary: Kodiak -- The new line of pinks in a pouch being launched by Chicken of the Sea is good news for Alaska's struggling salmon industry, as virtually all of the fish will come from Alaska. The San Diego-based company said last week it would begin shipping Premium Pink salmon in 7.1-ounce vacuum-packed foil pouches to U.S. retailers in January. The company will support the launch of its new pink salmon pouches with a multimillion-dollar campaign. Chicken of the Sea pioneered skinless/boneless pink salmon in cans in 1985, and 80 percent share of that product is eaten in the United States, said marketing director Van Effner. He added the company buys millions of pounds of frozen pinks from Alaska each year for the canned pack, and it has already increased purchases to accommodate the new pouched…

title: Halibut fishery closes season short of quota
summary: Petersburg -- Ripples from the Sept. 11 terrorist attacks weakened prices at the end of Alaska's eight-month halibut season, which closed Nov. 15 with about 3 million pounds of the quota remaining. Statewide totals show season landings at 95 percent of the 58.5 million pound allocation. "We had some pretty poor weather the last month and a half or so. Some people felt they couldn't get out there to finish fishing their allocations," said Jessie Garrett of the National Marine Fisheries Service. More than 8.5 million pounds of halibut was delivered in Kodiak in 2001, including 162,000 pounds of halibut in the last week of the season. Season-end prices were in the range of $1.60 to $1.80 a pound in Kodiak for freezer-bound halibut, while payments ranged up to $2 a pound for fresh sales, accor…

title: Dozens of ground troops help get issues on the ballot
summary: William E. Burns' job is to gather signatures for ballot initiatives. But it's clear, as he approaches passers-by at Northway Mall, that above all else he is a salesman. He always makes eye contact, crouching if necessary to get on the right level. He colors petitions so he knows what to pitch and when. And he showers prospective signers with ma'ams, sirs and y'alls as a way to show respect. "It doesn't matter what you're doing in life. People size you up in a split second," he says. "If you don't have the courtesy to show them you're earnest and honest in what you're doing, you're not going to get the time of day from them." For the disillusioned, the angry and the fed-up, petitions offer a way to bypass the Legislature and go directly to the people. Burns, with his clipboards, pens and u…

title: Anchorage count finds surprises among 11,758 birds
summary: Among the thousands of birds spied during this year's Anchorage Audubon Christmas Bird Count were a couple of surprises, most notably a northern saw-whet owl and two northern pintails. The rusty brown and white owl measures just 7 to 8 1/2 inches from the tip of its bill to the tip of its tail. Its distinctive call -- a short, high "too too too" repeated in rapid succession -- makes it readily apparent during mating season, said count compiler David DeLap. However, it has been included in only one other Christmas Count here, in 1998. "They're a hard owl to find in the winter," DeLap said. Two northern pintails were seen in the Ship Creek area, the single record-breaker in this year's count. The duck has appeared 11 times in the count since 1974, but this marks the only time that two of the…

title: Export costs befuddle plan of LNG panel
summary: Fairbanks -- An oil industry group has dispersed after failing to find a cost-efficient way to export liquefied North Slope natural gas in tankers. Members of the group included Phillips Petroleum, BP, Foothills Pipe Line Ltd. and Marubeni Corp. Their mission when forming three years ago was to study how to transport natural gas reserves through a pipeline to Valdez or Nikiski. The gas would then be liquefied and placed on tankers for shipment to Asia, Mexico or the West Coast. Members of the group have returned to their respective companies, Phillips Petroleum spokeswoman Dawn Patience told the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner this week. title: Caught between conservation and development
summary: Washington -- Kathleen Clarke, President Bush's nominee to head the Bureau of Land Management, said the biggest challenge she will face will be balancing conservation with oil, gas and other economic development on federal land. "I don't believe we have the option of choosing between the demands of growth and the imperative of conservation. We must do both," she said Wednesday during a confirmation hearing before the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee. Clarke, director of the Utah Department of Natural Resources, is expected to be confirmed, but a vote likely will not occur for at least two weeks. She would become the first woman to head the BLM. summary: Washington -- All along, supporters have told Congress that drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge would have little impact on the environment. Here's the mantra: With sideways drilling and other advances, the oil beneath the 1.5 million-acre coastal plain can be tapped with a "footprint" on the surface no larger than 2,000 acres. To win support for ANWR drilling in the hours before the U.S. House vote on energy legislation, Rep. John Sununu, R-N.H., put the promise in the bill, saying the "surface acreage covered by production and support facilities" must not exceed 2,000 acres. The final, resounding vote, shortly after midnight on Aug. 2, surprised even some drilling supporters: 240-189 in favor of the energy bill and its contentious provision to open ANWR. summary: Fairbanks -- Sen. Ted Stevens and Rep. Don Young want to redesign the way mail is handled in the Bush. Other carriers that use both large and small planes say changes are necessary and that the congressional delegation's proposals would improve service in the Bush. Last week, some of the smaller air carriers sent out a detailed media packet objecting to the proposals. title: Tlingits hoped for more than religion from Haines mission
summary: Juneau -- It was more than religion that prompted the Tlingits around Haines to request a mission be established in their community, according to researcher Debbie McBride. The University of Alaska Southeast senior has written a paper on the first five years of the Haines Mission, which was founded by Presbyterian missionaries in 1881. title: A passion for the people of Israel
summary: Wasilla resident Deborah Schneider first journeyed to Israel in 1998 as part of a tourist group. A highlight of the trip was standing at the summit of Masada (Hebrew for "fortress") on the western side of the Judean Desert. According to Jewish tradition, 960 Jewish patriots committed suicide there in A.D. 73 rather than surrender to Roman troops. Masada's cliffs rise 1,400 feet. Its flat top is 1,900 feet long and 650 feet wide. "You can see the land of Israel for miles all around," said Schneider. "You can also see the Dead Sea." The group also visited the Western Wall of the Temple Mount in the Old City of Jerusalem. The Second Temple of Jerusalem stood there until the Romans destroyed it in A.D. 70. title: Permanent Fund checks likely to grow smaller
